steps.title: Read the graph data to find which gene protects against A. sicerae
steps.content:
Type A flies have DptA silenced but DptB active — they show roughly 90% survival against A. sicerae. Type B flies have DptB silenced but DptA active — survival drops to nearly 0%. Survival tracks the presence of DptB, not DptA. The passage also confirms DptB does not help against P. rettgeri, making its protection specific to A. sicerae.
  • Choice A — DptA confers defense against A. sicerae regardless of the presence of DptB.: Reverses the finding — it is DptB, not DptA, that provides defense. Type B flies (DptA active, DptB silenced) show near-zero survival. ✗
  • Choice B — DptB protects against only one bacteria species, whereas DptA protects against multiple species.: The data shows DptA does not protect against A. sicerae; no evidence supports multi-species protection for DptA. ✗
  • Choice C — DptB may have developed as a specific defense against A. sicerae.: Consistent with the data: DptB alone drives survival against A. sicerae but not P. rettgeri, suggesting species-specific evolution. ✓
  • Choice D — defense against A. sicerae is strongest when both DptA and DptB are present.: The experiment does not show a condition where both are simultaneously silenced or both are active against A. sicerae; this is not supported by the data shown. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the evolutionary inference
steps.content: DptB alone confers robust defense against A. sicerae but not against P. rettgeri, pointing to a species-specific adaptation. That supports the conclusion that DptB may have evolved specifically to combat A. sicerae. That is the correct answer.
